posts.caption: Angi sells the same "lead" to a dozen guys, then you race each other to the bottom on price. We did the math on what a booked job actually costs you. It's worse than you think. 🔧
Pricing a job isn't a number you pull out of the air. It's a number you can defend when the homeowner flinches. Here's how to build one that holds.
Two shops, same town, same price. One's got 12 reviews, the other's got 140. You already know who gets the call. Here's how to close that gap — free.
The slow season isn't bad luck. It's a marketing problem you can see coming a mile away. Here's how to flatten it out before it hits.

We made a podcast. It's called Get Booked, Not F***ed — two old tradesmen and an idiot apprentice talking real shop marketing. No fluff, all bite, occasionally educational. Listen free. 🎧 @PlayMorePods #PodcastsOnAmazonMusic
Most contractors think 'marketing' is a logo and a boosted post. It's not. It's a machine: get found, get picked, get booked. Everything else is decoration. 🔧
Quoting, in one sentence. What would you add to the list? 👇
Your $35 lead isn't $35. Once you count the ones that never book, the real cost of a booked job is closer to $700. Here's the math nobody shows you.
Every shop has had this exact conversation. 🤝
Customers find you through 5 doors. If one lead app is most of your work, you don't own a business — you rent one. Spread out before they shut you off.
Cheap work isn't cheap. It's just paid for twice. Seen it?
There are 3 free spots at the top of every local search. Your Google Business Profile is the ticket in — and most contractors leave it half-empty.
What's the worst “the last guy did it” job you've walked into? Photos absolutely encouraged. We'll feature the best (worst) ones. 👇
98% of people leave a contractor website without calling. It's not the colors — it's that the phone number isn't a giant tap-to-call button. Fix that.
Found behind the drywall this week: a garden hose doing a P-trap's job. Rate the previous install 1–10. I'll start — it's on fire. 🔥
Before a customer ever hears your voice, they've judged you on your reviews. 140 recent reviews beats 12 — even at a higher price.
Drop the runner-up below. 👇
Should you pay for leads? Sometimes gold (LSA), sometimes a footrace (Angi). The test is the same: cost per BOOKED job, never the sticker price.
Plumbers, you're up first. The rest of us are not ready. 👇
Most contractors who 'tried Google Ads' just never answered the phone. Answer every call, start tiny, kill the losers. Here's how to not get burned.
It's somewhere in the truck. It's always somewhere in the truck.
A third of your future customers won't Google you — they'll ask ChatGPT who to hire. Good news: the same foundation wins both.
Every jobsite has “that guy.” What's his signature move? 👇
Five numbers, once a month, makes you impossible to fleece: cost per lead, cost per booked job, close rate, review count, response time.
Settle it in the comments. We're not picking sides. (Yes we are.) 👇
You don't lose most jobs on price. You lose them on response time. The first contractor to call back usually wins — even if he's worse than you.

Slow-season survival rule #1: the time to market is when you're busy, not when the phone stops. The work you book in summer is what fills January. What are you doing now to stay booked through winter? 👇
Your cheapest leads aren't on Angi. They're the customers you already made happy. Most contractors never ask — then complain leads are expensive.

Three ways shops bleed money they never see:

1. Unbilled change orders
2. “Quick favors” that aren't quick
3. Not charging for the drive

Which one's getting you this year? 👇
New episode of Get Booked, Not F***ed: why the guy with 140 reviews beats you even when he's worse — plus round 2 of Porno or Plumbing. 🎧 @PlayMorePods #PodcastsOnAmazonMusic

Posted for a helper. Three no-shows and one guy who quit because I asked him to sweep. The trades don't have a wage problem — they have a “show up” problem. Who's actually hiring right now? Drop your city. 👇
New episode: you don't lose jobs on price, you lose them on response time. 'Answer Your Phone' — speed to lead, plus a roofing company gets roasted. 🎧 @PlayMorePods #PodcastsOnAmazonMusic
Drop your best before/after. Any trade — roof, panel, re-pipe, install, cleanout. Let's see the work that doesn't get enough credit. 👇
78% of homeowners hire whoever calls back FIRST — not the best, not the cheapest. The first. (Lead Connect.) If you let calls ring, you're handing jobs to your competitor.
Respond to a lead in 5 minutes and you're 100x more likely to qualify it than if you wait 30. (MIT Sloan.) Speed isn't a nice-to-have — it's the game.
What an Angi booked job ACTUALLY costs once you count the leads that never close: ~$542. Google LSA: ~$168. Same money, very different math. (2026 data.)
The average contractor website converts 2–3% — meaning ~98% leave without calling. (WebFX.) Your site has one job: make the call easy.
91% of people read your reviews before they call, and most won't consider you under 4 stars. (BrightLocal.) Your reputation sells before you pick up.
HVAC on Google LSA: $51/lead, 44% book, $2,110 avg ticket — 9.55x return. (SearchLight, 888 contractors.) The exclusive-lead math shared apps don't want you running.
35% of people now ask AI — ChatGPT, Gemini — who to hire, not Google. (2026.) Invisible to AI = invisible to a third of the market.
1 in 7 calls to home-services businesses goes unanswered. (CallRail.) That's not a phone problem — it's a payroll problem.
Shared leads convert 6–10%. Your own organic leads: 18–24%. A direct call: up to 40%. (2026.) Cheap leads are the most expensive kind.
72% of contractors are upping their marketing budget in 2026 — and 62% say lead gen is their #1 challenge. (CallRail.) The question isn't whether to market. It's whether you do it smart.
Plumbing on Google LSA: $57/lead, 44.5% book, $1,714 ticket, 6.85x ROAS. (SearchLight.) Real numbers beat 'trust me' every time.
The US home-services market is over $524 BILLION a year, and 75% of pros expect to grow in 2026. There's plenty of work — winners are just easier to find.

Quick gut-check for any contractor buying leads: a booked job from Google Local Services Ads runs about $168. The same job from Angi/HomeAdvisor? About $542 (2026 lead-network comparisons). The difference is who else gets your lead — Angi sells it to 2-4 pros, Google sells it to you alone.

Got an email saying "I audited your site and found problems"? Delete it. If that marketer were so great at marketing, why are they cold-calling a plumber three states away? Real agencies get FOUND. They don't hunt you down with a form-letter audit. Ask them: where'd you find me, and name 3 clients in my trade. Watch them go quiet.

"Guaranteed #1 on Google in 30 days." Google itself says that's impossible — no one can guarantee a ranking spot. So when a guy promises one, he just told you everything you need to know about him. Real local SEO is months of honest work, not a 30-day miracle.

"Only 3 SEO spots left in your city — sign by Friday!" Nope. Google's rankings take months to move. Signing today vs next week changes nothing about your results. The only thing that countdown clock speeds up is money leaving your pocket. If it's a good deal today, it's a good deal next week.

"We found 47 critical errors on your website!" Relax. A robot made that list in 4 seconds and would send the same one to every contractor in town. Missing alt tags and meta descriptions don't move your rankings — they're bait for a monthly retainer. The real tell? It never names the competitors actually beating you.

That "local in-house team" your marketing company brags about? Sometimes it's a few contractors overseas, marked up big, sold to you as senior American talent. Outsourcing isn't the crime — hiding it and overcharging for it is. Here's how to catch a white-label markup shop before you sign.

That extra plumber on the map near you? It might not exist. Some agencies build fake Google listings to catch your calls and resell them to the highest bidder. You end up buying back work that was always yours. Here's how to spot a ghost pin and report it.

"Free demo" for your contracting business? The demo's free. The 12-month auto-renewing contract they slide under you at the end is not. Before you sign ANYTHING, ask one question: "If I cancel next month, what do I keep?" If the answer is nothing, you're renting, not buying.

The agency retainer is never the real cost. On top of it you're paying a setup fee, ad spend that goes straight to Google (not you), and a contract that locks you in. The only number that matters? Cost per booked job. Google LSA books one for ~$168 exclusive. Angi/HomeAdvisor? ~$542, shared with up to 4 other pros (SearchLight 2026).

Got $10K to spend on marketing? Don't blow it on ads yet. Fix your Google profile and reviews first — 91% of homeowners read reviews and most skip anyone under 4 stars (BrightLocal 2025). Then build a site that books jobs. THEN turn on ads. Order matters. Here's the math.

Real talk for contractors: an Angi booked job runs about $542 (2026 data) and you share that lead with 4-5 other pros. A 30-second Reel of you fixing a slab leak? Costs you 20 minutes and reaches strangers in your town who never followed you. That's the trade worth making in 2026.

Quick gut check for contractors: stop asking what a lead costs. Ask what a BOOKED JOB costs. Same plumbing job runs about $168 on Google LSA but about $542 on Angi/HomeAdvisor (2026 lead-network data) — because LSA is exclusive and Angi sells your lead to 2–4 other pros. One formula, and nobody can fleece you again.

Real talk for contractors: SEO doesn't ring the phone next week. Google ranks you on trust, and trust takes months to earn. It's the cheapest job source over time (~$290-310 per booked job, 2026 comparisons) — but you can't wait around. Run Local Services Ads to book jobs NOW and answer every call in 5 minutes. 78% of homeowners hire whoever responds first (Lead Connect 2026).

Yelp for contractors: yes or no? Honest answer — claim the FREE listing (91% read reviews, BrightLocal 2025), but think hard before buying the ads. The cheapest booked job out there is Google LSA at ~$168 and it's exclusive (SearchLight 2026). Make Yelp beat that or skip it.

HVAC owners, real talk: a "lead" and a "booked job" are not the same thing. Per 2026 lead-network comparisons, a booked job costs ~$168 on Google LSA, ~$250 on Thumbtack, and ~$542 on Angi/HomeAdvisor — same homeowner, wildly different price. The difference? Exclusive vs. shared leads.

Thumbtack isn't ripping you off — it's just math. Your lead gets sold to 4-5 pros, shared leads close at only 6-10% (2026 figures), and that's how a cheap contact becomes ~$250 per booked job (2026 lead-network comparisons). The fix is free: answer first. 78% of homeowners hire whoever responds first (Lead Connect 2026).

Angi and HomeAdvisor are the SAME company. And that same booked job that costs ~$168 on Google LSA? It runs about $542 through Angi/HomeAdvisor (2026 lead-network comparisons). The lead gets sold to 2-4 pros at once, so you're racing other contractors for something you already paid for.

Your marketing guy won't give you the login to your own Google Analytics? That's not him protecting you from "the technical stuff." That's a hostage situation. You own the accounts. He gets invited in as a guest you can kick out — never the other way around.

Nobody tells contractors this: WordPress is a back office, not a salesman. It collects leads and waits while you're under a sink. Meanwhile 78% of homeowners hire the FIRST contractor to respond (Lead Connect 2026). Your site shouldn't slow you down — it should get you the call.

Followers don't pay your light bill. A plumber with 10,000 followers and one with 200 eat the same if neither one's phone rings. The number that actually matters? Cost per booked job. Google LSA runs ~$168 per booked job; Angi/HomeAdvisor ~$542 (2026 lead comparisons). Likes aren't on that list for a reason.

Plumbers — a $57 lead and a $542 lead can both make you exactly $0. The difference? 78% of homeowners hire the FIRST contractor who responds (Lead Connect 2026). Your best marketing move this week costs nothing: answer the phone faster.

Electricians, here's a real number: your Google leads cost about $39 each on Local Services Ads per SearchLight 2026 — the cheapest in the trades. HVAC pays $51, plumbing $57. 43.4% book at a $1,434 job. That's an 8.52x return. The only way to blow it? Not answering the phone.

Roofers: that $79 Google lead (LocaliQ 2025) isn't your real problem. Your website is. Roofing sites convert just 3-7% (WebFX 2026), so 9 of every 10 clicks you paid for walk right out the door. Fix the leak before you blame the price.

One-truck contractor? You don't need 6 social accounts. You need 2 done right. Google Business Profile is where buyers actually look — per CallRail 2026, 81% of homeowners rely on Google reviews to decide who to hire. Add a Facebook page for local groups. Skip TikTok and X. Use a real photo, keep your name/address/phone identical everywhere, and answer the phone fast. That's the whole game. Full guide at booked-job.com.

Want to know who's getting the calls in your area? It's not the best contractor — it's the one with the most reviews. 91% of people read reviews before hiring, and most skip anyone under 4 stars (BrightLocal 2025). The good news: your first 50 reviews are already in your phone. Every past customer you never asked. Here's the exact text to send and when: https://booked-job.com/blog/get-your-first-50-reviews/

No ad budget yet? Good news: the stuff that wins your first 30 days is free. 78% of homeowners hire the FIRST contractor to respond (Lead Connect 2026). Answer the phone fast, fill out your Google Business Profile, and ask every happy customer for a review. That's the whole month-one plan. Read it: https://booked-job.com/blog/marketing-warm-up-tiny-budget/

Picking your ad platform by gut? Stop. The rule is start where intent is highest. Google LSA books jobs at about $168 each because the lead is exclusive and ready to hire (SearchLight Digital 2026). Google Search catches the big jobs LSA misses. Meta is for demand you create, not catch. And none of it matters if you don't pick up: 78% of homeowners hire the first contractor to respond (Lead Connect 2026). Full breakdown on the blog.

Your first Google Search ad isn't about getting clicks. It's about the phone ringing with a job you actually want. Go tight on keywords (service + your town), send the click to a page that matches, and ANSWER every call. 14% of home-service calls go missed (CallRail 2026), and 78% of homeowners hire whoever responds first (Lead Connect 2026). Full no-hype guide: booked-job.com/blog/your-first-google-search-ad/

Your first Facebook ad isn't going to ring the phone this week — and that's not a bug. Google ads catch people who ALREADY want the work (great for emergency plumbing, HVAC, electrical). Facebook ads plant seeds in people who aren't ready yet. Both work. They just work on different clocks. New to ads? Start with Google for the ready buyers, then add Meta for your before-and-afters. Here's the plain-money breakdown → https://booked-job.com/blog/your-first-meta-facebook-ad/

A truck wrap is a billboard that drives to the job. Here's the part the wrap shop won't tell you: it builds memory, it doesn't ring your phone today. The wraps that actually get calls have a big readable number, ONE trade, and your town — not six services in tiny font and a URL nobody can type at 40mph. And if you want to know whether it works? Put a dedicated tracking number on the truck. No tracking number, no real answer. A guess is not a number.

That billboard on the highway feels great to see — your name up in lights, your buddies texting you. But here's the cold question: did it book a single job? Most contractor billboards are trophies, not lead sources. The fix is dead simple: put a dedicated tracking phone number on it that lives NOWHERE else. Every call to that number came from the board. Now you can actually do the math. No tracking number, no proof. Full breakdown at booked-job.com.

Old-school still works. A vanity number on your truck is a billboard you pay for ONCE, then it books calls for years with no per-lead fee. Direct mail still lands too. But here's the rule: put a unique tracking number on every mailer, or you'll never know if it made you a dime. And answer the phone, home-service shops miss 14% of calls (CallRail 2026). Full playbook on the blog.

That swirly AI flyer you posted? Your customer has seen it 500 times this week. Generic AI content blends in — and the average contractor site already loses 98% of visitors without a single call (WebFX 2026). The fix isn't fancier. It's realer: your job, your photo, your street name. AI polishes the words. You bring the truth.

Tired of typing the same review replies, captions, and customer answers every week? We put together copy-paste AI prompts built for contractors — and the one trick that makes them sound like a real tradesperson, not a marketer. (Hint: you feed the AI your own words first.) 88% of buyers favor businesses that respond to all reviews — CallRail 2026. Link in comments.

Your customer isn't calling because they're excited. They're calling because something broke and they're scared of two things: the broken thing, and getting ripped off by whoever shows up. 78% hire the FIRST contractor to respond (Lead Connect 2026). Not the cheapest. The first one who answers. Are you answering? https://booked-job.com/blog/why-home-service-customers-buy/

The reason you lost that job probably had nothing to do with your price. 91% of homeowners read reviews before they decide (BrightLocal 2025), and 78% hire the FIRST contractor to respond (Lead Connect 2026). Strong reviews get you picked. Answering first gets you booked. If you're doing one without the other, you're leaking jobs. Get found, get picked, get booked. booked-job.com

Hate marketing? Same. Here's the whole plan for a one-truck shop: 3 hours, once a month. Hour 1 — fix your Google profile and add fresh job photos. Hour 2 — reply to your reviews and text happy customers for new ones. Hour 3 — set up a way to answer leads in 5 minutes. That's it. 78% of homeowners hire the first contractor to respond (Lead Connect 2026), so speed beats a fancy website every time. Full plan on the blog.

Your jobsite photos feel fake because you took them AFTER you cleaned everything up. That's a brochure — people scroll right past it. Next job, take the BEFORE shot too. The rusty pipe. The scary panel. The soaked attic. That's the part a homeowner looks at and goes "that's exactly my problem." That's what books the job. No script. No fancy camera. Just point your phone at the work you're already standing in front of.

Real talk for contractors: the cheapest lead you'll ever get is the yard sign at your last job. The neighbors can SEE your work — that's trust you can't buy on Google. But here's the move almost nobody makes: put a call-TRACKING number on the sign instead of your main line. Now you know exactly which signs and which streets ring the phone. Full guide → booked-job.com/blog/do-yard-signs-still-work-for-contractors/

Quick question: which of your yard signs booked a job last month? If you can't answer that, you're not alone — but you are flying blind. Online leads come with a dashboard built in. Google LSA even shows ~$168 per booked job (SearchLight 2026). Your truck wrap, your signs, your door hangers? They tell you nothing — unless you tag them. Give each one its own phone number or QR code and finally see what's working. Here's how: booked-job.com

AI won't crawl your attic or answer your phone. But it WILL write every Google review reply in 5 minutes instead of a whole evening, and 88% of homeowners favor businesses that respond to all their reviews (CallRail 2026). That's the real use for AI in a service business. Everything else is mostly hype. Plain-English breakdown for plumbers, HVAC, roofers and GCs: https://booked-job.com/blog/ai-101-for-contractors/

Real talk on Google Local Services Ads: for most trades, it's a yes. They book a job at about $168 each, the cheapest lead source out there, and the lead is YOURS, not shared with 4-5 other pros like Thumbtack and Angi (SearchLight 2026). HVAC pros are seeing 9.55x ROAS. The one thing that kills it? A slow phone. 78% of homeowners hire whoever answers first (Lead Connect 2026). Fix that, then turn on the ads.

The marketing trend nobody in the trades is talking about: 35% of homeowners now START with AI tools like ChatGPT, not Google (2026). If your shop isn't in the AI's answer, you don't exist for that customer. Good news — the fix is boring and free-ish: clear answers on your site, an accurate Google profile, and reviews. Get found, get picked, get booked.

General contractors pay the MOST per lead of any trade: about $94 for one Google Search lead (LocaliQ 2025 / CallRail). And GC websites convert the worst at 3.65% (WebFX 2026). Most expensive clicks plus worst conversion equals money walking out the door. The fix is not cheaper leads. It is answering every call and giving homeowners a reason to pick you. Full breakdown on the blog.

Painters, real talk: that $25 Angi lead is not a $25 problem. The number that actually matters is cost per BOOKED job — and for painters that's $180-450, per PCA 2026. Why so high? Most leads are shared (Thumbtack sends one lead to 4-5 pros, per 2026) and shared leads only convert at 6-10%. You burn a stack of cheap leads to land one job. We did the full math → https://booked-job.com/blog/painting-contractor-marketing-numbers/

We scraped the contractors actually ranking on Google Maps. They don't have 20-30 reviews — they have hundreds. Median: HVAC 519, plumbing 337, roofing 144, electrical 64 (our own 2026 data). If you've got 18 reviews and wonder why you're invisible, that's your answer.
Real data: we scraped the HVAC contractors actually ranking on Google Maps. Median review count? 519. (Booked Job scrape, 2026.) Most shops have a tenth of that — which is exactly the opening.
The plumbers ranking on Google Maps have a median of 337 reviews (our own 2026 scrape). Think 30 is enough? The data says otherwise. Here's the bar by trade.
